bull_thesis:
Oklo is the highest-profile pure-play on the single hardest physical bottleneck in the AI scale-out: firm, around-the-clock, carbon-free baseload power for hyperscale data centers. The bull case rests on five pillars.

First, a credible regulatory lead with real dates behind it. The Department of Energy approved a Nuclear Safety Design Agreement, the Nuclear Regulatory Commission accepted Oklo's preliminary design certification topical report in 15 days against a 30-to-60-day norm, the Idaho National Laboratory broke ground in September 2025, the company received its Environmental Compliance Permit, and an accelerated DOE Reactor Pilot Program pathway is targeting criticality milestones in 2026.

Second, a marquee demand pipeline. Oklo holds roughly 14 gigawatts of letters of intent — 12 GW with Switch, a letter of intent with Equinix, and a 1.2 GW agreement with Meta for a clean-energy campus in southern Ohio (part of Meta's broader push for up to 6.6 GW of nuclear power). This validates that the largest technology companies will commit to power years before it is delivered.

Third, a business model built for recurring revenue. The build-own-operate structure — where Oklo owns the reactor and sells electricity under long-term contracts (known as power purchase agreements, or PPAs) — generates repeat, high-switching-cost income rather than one-time reactor sales. Two additional optionality legs sit alongside it: a near-term medical and industrial radioisotope business (the Groves facility was built in 229 days and is targeting criticality on July 4, 2026, with a first commercial isotope contract pending) and a vertically integrated nuclear fuel position through a letter of intent with Centrus for up to five Aurora reactor cores, plus access to government plutonium and down-blended uranium.

Fourth, a strong balance sheet. Oklo holds roughly $2.5 billion in cash and marketable securities ($1.6 billion in cash, $0.9 billion in marketable securities), bolstered by a $1.2 billion at-the-market equity raise in the first quarter of 2026. That provides multi-year runway through first power — targeted for late 2027 at the Idaho National Laboratory — without a near-term solvency risk.

Fifth, credible technology partnerships. Collaborations with NVIDIA, Los Alamos National Laboratory, and INL's Prometheus AI program reinforce the AI-power connection.

Management describes the story as having moved decisively from strategy to execution. If Aurora deploys roughly on schedule and even a fraction of the 14 GW in letters of intent converts to priced, binding power purchase agreements, the long-run value of the fleet far exceeds today's $10.7 billion market cap — and the stock is already down roughly 67% from its high, meaning much of the peak enthusiasm premium has been worked off.
bear_thesis:
Oklo carries a $10.7 billion market cap on zero revenue. Its entire value is a discounted option on a fleet that does not yet exist, and the bear case is that the option is still priced for near-certain success the company cannot yet underwrite.

The roughly 14 GW pipeline is entirely non-binding letters of intent — expressions of interest without signed contracts, fixed pricing, or payment obligations. Conversion rates are unproven, and the largest technology companies are deliberately spreading their bets: Meta's nuclear commitment spans Oklo, Vistra, and TerraPower simultaneously.

Structural share dilution is baked in. The share count stands at roughly 174 million, up about 28% year over year; the quarter was funded by a $1.2 billion at-the-market equity raise; and the company guides to $350 to $450 million of investing cash use per year. Each year between now and first power means more equity issuance and a shrinking value per share. The $2.5 billion of cash on the balance sheet was itself produced by heavy dilution.

Timeline slippage risk is real and has already happened. The original Aurora-2027 framing has been recalibrated; first commercial operations is now targeted for late 2027 at Idaho National Laboratory, and commercial Nuclear Regulatory Commission licensing for the broader fleet has not yet been submitted. Any further slip pushes terminal cash flows further out and reduces the option's value.

A material portion of the bull narrative relies on a politically supplied tailwind — the May 2025 executive order creating the DOE Reactor Pilot Program and its July 4, 2026 criticality deadline. That is powerful, but it is regime-dependent.

On management credibility: the chief executive has been a net seller of roughly $10 million of personal stock over the past twelve months, even as the public narrative is maximally optimistic.

Competition across the small modular reactor field — NuScale, X-energy, Kairos, TerraPower — combined with the first-of-a-kind engineering and regulatory execution risk of a reactor design that has never been commercially deployed means the probability-weighted path to a profitable fleet is wide and uncertain.

With no revenue, no margins, and no returns, there is no fundamental margin of safety. The only cushion is sentiment — and sentiment in pre-revenue nuclear is violently mean-reverting. The stock round-tripped from roughly $45 to $194 and back to $61 inside a single year.
sop_bull:
Roughly $95 to $110 per share, using a probability-weighted long-run discounted cash flow (DCF — the present value of all future cash flows) on the full company, with no separate business-line segments to report yet.

Powerhouse fleet (the core value): assume roughly 3 to 4 GW of the 14 GW pipeline converts to deployed build-own-operate capacity by the early 2030s, at approximately $1.5 to $2.0 million of recurring operating earnings per megawatt under long-term power purchase agreements, discounted heavily for execution, timeline, and dilution risk. That produces roughly $13 to $16 billion of equity value attributable today, or about $75 to $92 per share on roughly 174 million shares — with the bull case assuming project financing and DOE loans fund most of the $350 to $450 million annual build cost, reducing the need for further share issuance.

Radioisotope business: a credible near-term revenue stream (Groves online around mid-2026, first contract pending) worth roughly $1 to $2 billion, or about $6 to $11 per share.

Nuclear fuel and recycling optionality (Centrus letter of intent for five cores, access to government plutonium and uranium inventories): roughly $1 to $1.5 billion of strategic moat value, or about $6 to $9 per share.

Net cash of $2.5 billion is already embedded in the equity.

Summing these: roughly $95 to $110 per share — implying about 55% to 80% upside if execution lands.
sop_bear:
Roughly $28 to $40 per share, using the same whole-company option framework but with severe execution and dilution haircuts.

Powerhouse fleet: discount the letters of intent to a low single-digit conversion probability and push first meaningful cash flow into the 2030s. Risk-adjusted equity value of roughly $3 to $4 billion divided across a share count that could reach 210 to 240 million after two to three more years of at-the-market and other equity issuance: roughly $17 to $23 per share.

Radioisotope business: still nascent and sub-scale with no contract signed: roughly $0.5 to $1 billion, or about $2 to $4 per share.

Fuel optionality: still letter-of-intent grade, deserves minimal credit: roughly $0 to $1 per share.

Net cash of $2.5 billion (roughly $11 to $12 per share today, eroding as cash burns).

Sum: roughly $28 to $40 per share — implying about 35% to 55% downside from current levels if the market re-prices this as a pre-revenue, dilution-funded option to its cash-plus-modest-optionality floor.
sop_breakdown:
Whole-company valuation using a sum-of-optionality (valuing each part of the business separately) approach — no reportable revenue segments exist yet.

(1) Powerhouse fleet: long-run discounted cash flow, probability-weighted at roughly 35% to 45% success on about 3 gigawatts deployed by the early 2030s under build-own-operate power purchase agreements, discounted for first-of-a-kind execution risk and the late-2027 first-power slip risk. Value: roughly $6 to $8 billion, or about $35 to $46 per share.

(2) Radioisotope business (Groves facility targeting criticality July 4, 2026; first commercial contract pending; revenue possible starting 2026): roughly $1 to $1.5 billion, or about $6 to $9 per share.

(3) Nuclear fuel and recycling vertical-integration optionality (Centrus letter of intent, government plutonium and uranium access): roughly $0.5 to $1 billion, or about $3 to $6 per share.

(4) Net cash: $2.5 billion today (roughly $14 per share), but burning roughly $430 to $550 million per year all-in; crediting approximately $1.8 to $2.2 billion after two years of burn, or about $10 to $13 per share.

Overlays: at-the-market and other equity issuance (share count rising from roughly 174 million) is an ongoing per-share headwind already partly reflected. A potential DOE loan or project-financing facility would reduce equity dilution and represents upside not in the base case.

Base-case sum: roughly $52 to $62 per share, midpoint roughly $57 — essentially in line with the spot price of roughly $60.88. The market is already paying close to a reasonable probability-weighted intrinsic value, and the asymmetry is roughly two-sided.
risk_reward:
Roughly symmetric-to-slightly-unfavorable from the current price of roughly $60.88. The base-case midpoint of about $57 implies you are not buying at a discount today — you are paying close to fair value for a wide-distribution option. The upside to the bull case (roughly $100) is about 65%; the downside to the bear floor (roughly $34) is about 44%.

The reward-to-risk only turns clearly favorable on a meaningful pullback toward the high-$40s to low-$50s range — the level anchored by net cash plus the radioisotope business — which is precisely the forced-selling-pressure setup this position is waiting for.
catalyst_status: SOFT_EXTENDED. The load-bearing catalysts are (a) the DOE Reactor Pilot Program / July-4-2026 criticality milestone (driven by the May-2025 Trump executive order) and the Groves isotope facility target criticality July 4 2026 with first commercial isotope contract 'pending'; (b) Aurora-INL first commercial operations targeted LATE 2027; (c) conversion of the ~14 GW non-binding LOIs (Switch 12 GW, Meta 1.2 GW, Equinix) into binding priced PPAs; (d) a potential DOE loan / project-finance facility. As of June 2026 these are real and dated but NON-BINDING and serially forward-referenced: the LOIs carry no payment obligation, first power is 18+ months out and has slipped before, and the July-4 criticality is a test/isotope-criticality milestone tied to a single policy regime, not a commercial-revenue inflection. No hard binary, deal-fixed (ARB), or already-spent (FIRED) catalyst with locked asymmetry in the next 90 days. Verified live (June 2026 sources): Q1-2026 10-Q, World Nuclear News / NEI on the July-4-2026 criticality deadline, Utility Dive on the Meta multi-developer nuclear deals, Oklo/Centrus 8-K on the HALEU LOI.
peer_comps_note:
True peers within the scale-out power-generation / small modular reactor developer group (all verified as of June 21, 2026): Oklo at roughly $10.66 billion market cap (price roughly $60.88, roughly 174 million shares, $0 revenue, $2.5 billion cash); NuScale Power at roughly $4.28 billion (pre-revenue, utility-anchored customer mix, down 39% year to date, 52-week range $8.85 to $57.42); Nano Nuclear Energy at roughly $1.2 to $1.5 billion (price roughly $28.40, pre-revenue microreactor designs, 52-week range $18.93 to $60.87).

Broader power-generation peers that do have cash flows and AI-power exposure include Vistra, Constellation Energy, Talen Energy, and GE Vernova — these trade on real operating earnings and represent the 'buy power now' alternative that hyperscalers are also signing. Meta's nuclear commitment spans Oklo, Vistra, and TerraPower simultaneously.

On every conventional metric — revenue, margins, return on invested capital, free cash flow — Oklo is not rankable against the cash-generating names and is valued purely on option and pipeline terms. Within the pre-revenue small modular reactor cohort, Oklo carries the richest valuation (roughly 2.5 times NuScale on market cap, roughly seven to nine times Nano Nuclear) — a premium justified by the density of AI and hyperscaler letters of intent (Switch, Meta, Equinix versus NuScale's utility tilt), the DOE regulatory lead, the $2.5 billion war chest, and the near-term isotope revenue leg. But it also makes Oklo the most expensive way to own the small modular reactor theme and the most exposed to a sentiment de-rating. Oklo ranks first on AI capital-expenditure exposure and balance-sheet strength, first (most expensive) on valuation, and tied with peers at zero on margins and returns. Oklo leads the pre-revenue cohort on pipeline gigawatts.
role_in_scaleout:
Oklo's role is at the power-generation root of the AI build-out stack — providing the firm, dispatchable, carbon-free baseload electricity that every hyperscaler campus ultimately needs and is structurally short of. Its specific differentiation is the build-own-operate power-purchase-agreement model combined with vertical nuclear fuel integration, aimed directly at data-center customers (Switch, Meta, Equinix) rather than traditional utilities.

The underlying demand is highly durable in direction — AI power scarcity is a multi-year structural problem that is worsening. But Oklo's capture of that demand is not yet durable. The company is pre-revenue, first power is targeted for late 2027, the pipeline is non-binding, and the largest technology companies can and do buy firm power from already-operating alternatives — gas, existing nuclear, Vistra, Constellation, Talen — while Oklo builds.
consensus_delta:
Where we disagree with the market on Oklo cuts both ways — this is not a clean case where the street is clearly offside.

On the optimistic side, the market may undervalue two things: first, the radioisotope business as a genuine near-term revenue stream — Groves is expected online around mid-2026 with a first commercial contract pending, which would partially remove the binary all-or-nothing character of the story; second, the possibility of a DOE loan or project-financing facility that would fund the $350 to $450 million annual build cost with debt rather than equity, sharply reducing the dilution that bears extrapolate forward.

On the cautious side, the bulls may undervalue three things: first, the roughly 14 GW pipeline is discussed as though it were contracted backlog when it is entirely non-binding letters of intent with no pricing or payment obligation, and the largest technology companies are deliberately multi-sourcing; second, the per-share denominator is a moving target — even a correct forecast of total fleet value can disappoint on a per-share basis as the share count rises through at-the-market raises; third, the DOE and July 4 tailwind is regime-dependent and could lose momentum.

Net: Oklo is a fairly-priced wide-distribution option where the asymmetry is roughly two-sided at the current price, not a situation where consensus is clearly and correctable wrong in one direction.
valley_of_death:
The risky window over the next three to nine months has four main hazards.

First, continued zero-revenue quarters with widening losses. The first quarter of 2026 showed a net loss of $33.1 million and an operating loss of $51.2 million, with 2026 guidance pointing to $80 to $100 million of operating cash use plus $350 to $450 million of investing cash use. Every quarter is a burn quarter with no offsetting revenue — and even if isotope revenue begins in 2026, it will be immaterial relative to a $10.7 billion market cap.

Second, dilution overhang. The $1.2 billion at-the-market raise in the first quarter of 2026 signals the company will keep tapping equity markets. Any further issuance is a direct, mechanical headwind to per-share value and a sentiment negative.

Third, milestone-slip risk. The July 4, 2026 Groves criticality target and the late-2027 first-power date are the visible checkpoints. First-of-a-kind nuclear timelines slip routinely — a slip on either would puncture the 'moving from strategy to execution' narrative.

Fourth, macro and positioning risk. Oklo is a high-beta, retail-heavy, AI-spend-correlated momentum stock (the share price round-tripped from roughly $45 to $194 to roughly $61 in a year). In the current environment — where AI capital expenditure is the market's load-bearing wall and positioning is stretched — any signal of hyperscaler budget restraint or broad de-risking would hit Oklo harder than the cash-generating power companies. The thesis can be cut in half by a sentiment shift well before any operational milestone arrives.
positioning_washout:
Oklo's shareholder base is heavily retail and momentum-driven — built on the Sam Altman association, the 'AI nuclear' theme, and a dramatic price round-trip over the past year. This is fast-money ownership, not a yield- or dividend-anchored base that liquidates on a scheduled event.

The forced-selling-pressure risk is a sentiment washout: if a milestone slips, an equity raise is announced, or AI capital expenditure fears spike, momentum investors and computer-driven trend-following strategies could flush the stock toward its net-cash-plus-isotope floor in the high-$40s to low-$50s in a sharp move disconnected from any change in fundamental value. There is no index-rebalancing or dividend-mandate forced-seller; the washout, if it comes, is a de-grossing event.

Importantly, that washout would be the buying opportunity rather than a thesis-killer. The $2.5 billion cash position means this would be a sentiment flush, not a solvency event.
forcing_function:
The catalyst that forces a re-rating, ranked by proximity and impact.

(1) July 4, 2026 — the DOE Reactor Pilot Program criticality milestone and the Groves isotope facility target criticality. A hit — or a credible first commercial isotope contract signing — would be a tangible execution proof point. A miss or slip would puncture the 'moving from strategy to execution' narrative.

(2) Conversion of any portion of the roughly 14 GW in letters of intent (Switch 12 GW, Meta 1.2 GW, Equinix) into a binding, priced power purchase agreement. This is the single event that would transform 'expressed interest' into 'contracted backlog' and force analysts to underwrite real revenue.

(3) Announcement of a DOE loan or project-financing facility — the cleanest positive surprise, because it would de-risk the dilution path without requiring a commercial revenue event.

(4) Late-2027 Aurora at Idaho National Laboratory first commercial operations — the ultimate re-rating event, but outside the near-term window.

Of these, (1) is the nearest hard date and (2) carries the highest impact but has no fixed date.
moderator_conclusion:
Oklo is a real, well-funded, best-in-class pure-play on the most fundamental physical bottleneck in the AI scale-out: firm, carbon-free, around-the-clock baseload power. It sits on the right side of every long-run secular trend — an energy-transition winner in a world structurally short of clean firm power — with no terminal-value erosion risk. The moat (the durable competitive advantage) is narrow and still being built, but it is genuinely widening through regulatory licensing lead, vertical nuclear fuel integration, and the recurring-revenue build-own-operate model. That earns it a place on the research list.

But on the positioning overlay and the expectations-arbitrage math, this is a 'wait for the wash-out' call, not a buy at the current price. Three things drive that judgment.

First, the catalysts are dated but soft — no contractual payout. The roughly 14 GW pipeline is entirely non-binding letters of intent; first power is 18-plus months away and has slipped before; the July 4 criticality milestone is tied to a single policy regime. There is no hard, deal-fixed asymmetry in the near-term window.

Second, the valuation math says the market is already paying close to fair value. The base-case midpoint of roughly $57 sits essentially at the current price of roughly $60.88, with about 65% upside to the bull and 44% downside to the bear — roughly two-sided, with no margin of safety. Within the pre-revenue small modular reactor cohort, Oklo also carries the richest valuation: roughly 2.5 times NuScale and seven to nine times Nano Nuclear.

Third, the entire thesis depends on a single shared factor — the durability of AI capital expenditure — that is already load-bearing for most of Basket E and much of the broader market. Concentrating in a zero-revenue, high-beta expression of that single factor when positioning is stretched increases risk without adding diversification.

Conclusion: watch-list for the wash-out, conviction 3 out of 5. The name is worth owning on a sentiment-driven pullback toward the high-$40s to low-$50s net-cash-plus-isotope floor. It is not worth buying at full freight today. Stripped of the AI and catalyst premium, you are paying close to fair — or slightly rich — for a pre-revenue option with continuous dilution and a moving per-share denominator. The roughly 67% drawdown from the peak is a de-hyping event, not a value event.
interrogator_dossier:
Oklo Inc. — Forensic Review
Prepared for the investment committee. Scale-out Basket E (Power Generation). All figures cross-referenced against the Q1 2026 earnings call (reported May 12, 2026), the Q1 2026 quarterly filing (Form 10-Q), and live market data as of June 21, 2026 (price roughly $60.88, market cap roughly $10.66 billion, roughly 174 million diluted shares, down roughly 67% from the 52-week high of $193.84).

Evidence note: Oklo is a pre-revenue company. There is no revenue, margin, return on invested capital, free cash flow, or backlog conversion trajectory to cross-reference in the conventional sense. The 'financials' here are a cash-runway-and-dilution story, and the 'narrative' is a regulatory and commercial-pipeline story. The forensic question is therefore reframed: do management's milestone claims map to verifiable, dated regulatory and customer events, and is the cash bridge to first revenue credible without ruinous dilution?


Narrative Arc

Oklo's story has migrated decisively from strategy to execution over the past year — management's own framing on the Q1 2026 call. The arc: at the time of its de-SPAC transaction in mid-2024 (via AltC, backed by Sam Altman), Oklo was a single-product story — the Aurora microreactor on a build-own-operate utility model. By 2025 the company broadened to three business units: powerhouses (the Aurora fleet), nuclear fuel recycling, and a near-term radioisotope business (Atomic Alchemy) introduced to pull revenue forward into 2026. The 2026 overlay made AI data-center power demand the dominant new driver: the letter-of-intent pipeline expanded to roughly 14 GW (Switch 12 GW, Equinix letter of intent, Meta 1.2 GW Ohio campus), with NVIDIA, Los Alamos, and INL-Prometheus AI partnerships layered on as strategic enablers. What has been quietly de-emphasized: the original simple sell-power-via-power-purchase-agreement utility model has become a far more capital-intensive, vertically integrated build-out requiring $350 to $450 million of investing cash in 2026 alone.


Claims Versus Financials

Management's claim of having moved from strategy to execution is substantiated on process milestones: Idaho National Laboratory groundbreaking in September 2025; Groves built in 229 days; Nuclear Regulatory Commission preliminary design certification topical report accepted in 15 days against a 30-to-60-day norm; DOE Nuclear Safety Design Agreement approved.

The roughly 14 GW pipeline is richer in rhetoric than in legal commitment — 12 GW (Switch) and the Equinix letter of intent remain non-binding with no payment obligation; revenue today is zero. The claim of revenue potential starting in 2026 from isotopes is unproven — the first commercial isotope contract is 'pending' and Groves targets criticality July 4, 2026. The Centrus fuel agreement (up to five Aurora cores, 'timescales shift to the left for the first time') is plausible but still letter-of-intent grade.

Liquidity is confirmed strong: $2.5 billion in cash and securities ($1.6 billion cash, $0.9 billion marketable), after a $1.2 billion at-the-market raise in Q1 2026 — but achieved by heavy share issuance (roughly 174 million shares, up roughly 28% year over year). Hard numbers: $0 revenue; Q1 net loss $33.1 million; operating loss $51.2 million; earnings per share negative $0.19 (in line with expectations). Full-year 2026 guidance: $80 to $100 million of operating cash use plus $350 to $450 million of investing cash use.


Tone and Confidence Trajectory

Rising and offensive. Chief executive Jacob DeWitte is consistently forward-leaning: 'Nuclear doesn't have to be slow or expensive; it can be done in different ways'; 'incredible momentum'; 'deploy faster and do more simultaneously.' Tone has grown more aggressive over the past year with no defensive pivot on costs or macro conditions. The risk is the opposite: tone running ahead of contracted cash flows. One counter-signal: the chief executive has been a net seller of roughly $10 million of personal stock over the trailing 12 months.


Guidance Credibility

Oklo offers milestone guidance, not financial guidance. On milestones, the delivery record is medium to high: Idaho National Laboratory groundbreaking, fast Groves construction, accelerated Nuclear Regulatory Commission acceptances, DOE Other Transaction Agreement and Nuclear Safety Design Agreement approvals are all verifiable. However, the timeline has slipped before — the original 'Aurora 2027' framing has been recalibrated to late-2027 first commercial operations at Idaho National Laboratory, and full Nuclear Regulatory Commission commercial licensing for the broader fleet has not yet been submitted. The one cash-flow item the company guides to (operating and investing cash use) tracked in range in Q1. Overall reliability: medium — process milestones are credible, but commercial-revenue and first-power dates carry real slip risk.


Analyst Pressure Points

Key questions softened rather than resolved: (a) when does revenue actually arrive and at what scale — answered 'isotopes in 2026, first power late-2027' but the magnitude versus a $10.7 billion market cap was left vague; (b) how many letters of intent convert to binding agreements and at what price — cited as momentum without a specific conversion expectation; (c) dilution path — the $1.2 billion at-the-market raise was framed as balance-sheet strength without ownership of the per-share impact; (d) Nuclear Regulatory Commission versus DOE pathway — the DOE pilot program is presented as an accelerant that sidesteps the harder commercial Nuclear Regulatory Commission license, which remains pre-submission for the broader fleet.


Green Flags and Red Flags

Green: $2.5 billion cash (multi-year runway, no near-term solvency risk); genuine dated regulatory acceleration (DOE Nuclear Safety Design Agreement, Nuclear Regulatory Commission preliminary design certification acceptance, Idaho National Laboratory groundbreaking, Environmental Compliance Permit); tier-one customer logos (Meta, Switch, Equinix) plus NVIDIA validating AI power demand; near-term isotope revenue optionality; fuel-flexibility moat in the making (high-assay low-enriched uranium fuel plus recycling plus plutonium inventories).

Red: $0 revenue versus $10.7 billion market cap; pipeline is non-binding letters of intent, not contracted backlog; structural dilution (roughly 174 million shares up roughly 28% year over year, at-the-market funding, $350 to $450 million annual burn); chief executive net selling roughly $10 million of stock; first-power date has slipped before and commercial Nuclear Regulatory Commission license is pre-submission; stock is down roughly 67% from its high yet still embeds enormous assumed future success.


Hidden Signals

Terminology is shifting from 'reactor company' to 'energy and fuel platform' — the three-unit framing serves both as genuine diversification and as a narrative hedge against Aurora slippage. The DOE pathway is becoming the hero of the story, increasingly anchored on the May 2025 executive order mandating three test-reactor criticalities by July 4, 2026 — a powerful but politically supplied, regime-dependent tailwind. The claim that fuel timescales are 'shifting to the left' on the Centrus agreement is the first explicit acceleration claim, but it remains letter-of-intent grade. The AI partnerships (NVIDIA, Prometheus, Los Alamos) are engineering and modeling collaborations, not power-offtake commitments — they reinforce the AI association without yet being commercial demand.


Capital-Allocation Assessment

Trajectory: strengthening — but from a pre-revenue base, meaning the option is becoming better-funded and more credible, not that an earnings base is compounding.

The single most important consideration before sizing a position: this is a pre-revenue, multi-year option priced at $10.7 billion with continued dilution baked in. Nearly all value sits in a long-run discounted cash flow that requires first power on the late-2027 schedule, letters of intent converting to priced power purchase agreements, and financing the $350 to $450 million annual build without crushing per-share value. There is no margin of safety in the fundamentals — only in how much optionality the 67% drawdown has already worked off.

Moat: none to narrow (prospective) — no deployed asset, returns, or margins yet certify a moat; the potential moat (regulatory-license lead, vertical fuel integration, recurring power-purchase-agreement switching costs) is real and specific but unbuilt. Moat trend: widening — regulatory lead, fuel agreements, and customer letters of intent accumulating. Secular threat: none — Oklo is a secular winner (firm carbon-free baseload is exactly what the AI build-out is short of). The risks are execution, dilution, timeline slip, and competition, not terminal-value erosion. The apparent cheapness versus the 52-week high is an execution-risk and de-hyping event, not evidence of a shrinking fundamental base.

Management credibility 3 of 5; trajectory strengthening; moat narrow but prospective; moat trend widening; secular threat none.
skeptic_kill_fact: No single fact was found that would invalidate the thesis. Every load-bearing number verifies against the Q1 2026 quarterly filing (period ending March 31, 2026): cash and securities of $2,536.9 million ($1,594.1 million cash plus $614.5 million current marketable securities plus $328.3 million non-current marketable securities) matches the claimed $2.5 billion; 173,867,839 shares outstanding (roughly 174 million, up roughly 28% year over year, confirmed); Q1 net loss $33.1 million and operating loss $51.2 million confirmed; $1.18 billion at-the-market program completed in Q1 at an average of roughly $96.95 per share. Pre-revenue, $0 top line, roughly $10.6 billion market cap, spot roughly $61 — all confirmed. The DOE Reactor Pilot July 4, 2026 criticality target and late-2027-to-early-2028 first-power date verify (driven by the Trump executive order, Other Transaction Agreement signed; first-of-a-kind slip risk is real). The thesis is a fairly-priced, two-sided pre-revenue option — the 'wait for the wash-out' verdict at conviction 3 survives adversarial scrutiny and is, if anything, conservative.
skeptic_corrections:
One load-bearing correction to the bear case: the debate repeatedly characterizes the entire roughly 14 GW pipeline — including the Meta agreement — as entirely non-binding letters of intent with no payment obligation. That is wrong for the Meta leg.

Per Oklo's own press release and the quarterly filing, the Oklo-Meta 1.2 GW Pike County, Ohio agreement (signed January 5, 2026) is a binding prepayment agreement under which Meta prepays for power and funds deployment — Oklo uses that funding to secure nuclear fuel and advance Phase 1. This is materially more committed than a letter of intent, and the bear thesis errs in denying that any payment mechanism exists. This correction cuts against the bear case.

(The Switch 12 GW does remain genuinely non-binding — confirmed as a 'nonbinding framework' — and Meta is explicitly multi-sourcing across Oklo, Vistra, and TerraPower, so concentration and conversion risk on the remaining gigawatts stands.)

One additional clarification: the first phase of the Meta project targets delivery 'as early as 2030' (full 1.2 GW by 2034), which is separate from and later than the late-2027 Idaho National Laboratory first-power date. The bull case's near-term cash-flow timing rests on the Idaho National Laboratory site, not on Meta.
skeptic_evidence: Oklo Inc. Form 10-Q for period ended 2026-03-31 (SEC EDGAR, accession 000162828026034095): cash & equivalents $1,594.1M, marketable securities $614.5M current + $328.3M non-current (total cash+securities $2,536.9M), net loss $33.1M, operating loss $51.2M, 173,867,839 shares outstanding, $1.18B 2025-ATM completed in Q1 at avg ~$96.95/sh — https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/0001849056/000162828026034095/oklo-20260331.htm

Utility Dive, Oklo-Switch 12 GW Master Power Agreement is a NONBINDING framework; Meta inks up to 6.6 GW across Oklo/Vistra/TerraPower (multi-sourcing confirmed) — https://www.utilitydive.com/news/oklo-aurora-smr-advanced-nuclear-reactor-supply-agreement-data-center-developer-switch/735933/

World Nuclear News / Utility Dive (2025): DOE Reactor Pilot Program targets criticality in 3 test reactors by July 4 2026 (Trump EO-driven, Aurora-INL among initial selections, OTA signed); Oklo 75-MW Aurora design, first commercial deployment late-2027 to early-2028 at INL — https://www.utilitydive.com/news/oklo-75-mw-reactor-design-smr-nuclear/743578/

scale.role: Highest-profile SMR pure-play; DOE pilot + July-4-2026 criticality milestone, ~14 GW of LOIs (Meta leg binding-prepay).
scale.rationale: Real dated regulatory lead and a wide-distribution option (DOE Reactor Pilot, July-4-2026 criticality target, INL first-power late-2027) — but pre-revenue, so a TACTICAL option not a CORE holding; size as an option, not a position. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS, and the correction cuts FOR the name (the Meta 1.2 GW leg is a BINDING prepayment agreement, more committed than an LOI), so a small ~6pt haircut. Best of the speculative SMRs (top of the pre-revenue band, just sub-35 by the pre-revenue rule); base ~$57 ~at spot, bull +65% / bear -44%.
scale.demand_durability: Low-now/high-optional — pre-revenue; the Meta leg is a binding prepay, the Switch 12 GW is non-binding; first ops ~late-2027.
